question_title: Will the IOM Missing Migrants Project record at least 900 Mediterranean migrant deaths in 2026 before April 2026?
summary:
The IOM Missing Migrants Project has recorded an unprecedented death toll in the Mediterranean for early 2026 - the "deadliest start to a year" since records began in 2014. As of late February 2026, the central Mediterranean alone has 567+ recorded dead/missing (as of Feb 20), with additional deaths in Western and Eastern Mediterranean routes bringing the all-Mediterranean total to an estimated ~630-670+. The question asks whether this reaches 900 by end of March.

With ~37 days remaining and needing roughly 230-270 more deaths to reach 900, the required daily rate (~6-7/day) is below the observed February pace (~7-9/day) and well below the January pace (~16/day). Additionally, backfilling of verified January "invisible shipwrecks" (380+ missing from 9 boats, Jan 14-21) will add to the recorded total. March typically sees increased crossings as weather improves, supporting sustained death rates.

The main risks are: uncertainty in the current total (could be lower than estimated), potential for pace to slow if conditions are unusually calm, and IOM data recording lag potentially preventing late-March incidents from appearing in the dataset by the April 1 resolution date. However, Metaculus typically waits for authoritative data to populate, and the needed daily rate is modest relative to observed rates. My simulations across different parameter assumptions range from 63-91%, with a balanced central estimate around 75%.

factors.description: Current YTD all-Mediterranean total already ~630-670+ (central Med 567 as of Feb 20 + Western/Eastern routes + Feb 21-22 incidents off Crete and Libya)
2026 is the deadliest start on record: 524 deaths by Feb 10 vs 219 in same period 2025 and 416 in 2016
Required daily rate (~6-7/day) is below observed February pace (~7-9/day), meaning threshold can be reached even with some slowdown
Backfilling of unverified January events (380+ missing from 9 boats Jan 14-21, bodies still washing ashore) will increase recorded total
March typically brings increased Mediterranean crossings as weather improves from winter, supporting sustained or higher death rates
IOM data recording lag (~10 days) means some late-March incidents may not be in dataset at resolution time (April 1)
February pace (~7-9/day) already lower than January (~16/day); pace could slow further if no major storm or mass casualty events
Uncertainty in current total - could be as low as ~580 if some cited figures double-count or include non-Mediterranean routes

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.75 (75% probability YES)**

Key findings:
- The Mediterranean has seen its deadliest start to any year since IOM began recording in 2014, with ~630-670+ dead/missing already recorded by late February
- The threshold of 900 requires only ~230-270 more deaths in ~37 remaining days, translating to ~6-7 deaths/day — below the observed February rate of ~7-9/day
- Backfilling of January "invisible shipwrecks" (380+ missing from 9 boats departing Tunisia Jan 14-21, many still unverified) will add substantially to the total
- Main downside risks: IOM recording lag at resolution time, uncertainty in current totals, and possibility of pace slowing if March is unusually calm
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the IOM Missing Migrants Project record at least 900 Mediterranean migrant deaths in 2026 before April 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42237)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to Yes if the IOM Missing Migrants Project incident dataset shows that the year-to-date sum of dead plus missing through the end of March 2026 for “Mediterranean” is at least 900.The primary source is the IOM Missing Migrants Project public site : https://missingmigrants.iom.int/region/mediterranean
background_info:
This tracks the scale of lethal migration risk in the Mediterranean in early 2026, which is relevant for humanitarian planning and policy response.
